topic: fifa world cup 2026 fever in bangladesh
headline_marked: FIFA President Infantino: [[We are not the kings of the world]]
headline: FIFA President Infantino: We are not the kings of the world
summary: Gianni Infantino urges calm over visa issues and ticket pricing as the World Cup approaches. He emphasizes FIFA's role as a sports organization, not a global authority.
details: FIFA President Gianni Infantino addressed concerns regarding visa issues and ticket prices on the eve of the World Cup. He urged stakeholders to 'chill and relax,' stating that FIFA, as a sports organization, cannot dictate immigration policies to host nations.

Infantino's comments came after a Somali referee was denied entry to the U.S. despite holding a valid visa. He acknowledged the unfortunate incident but stressed that FIFA is working behind the scenes to resolve such issues, emphasizing that immigration decisions ultimately rest with national authorities.

Despite controversies, Infantino expressed no regrets about the U.S. co-hosting the tournament, calling such issues 'normal for an event of this magnitude.' He highlighted Iran's participation as proof of FIFA's ability to navigate complex political landscapes and bring people together.

The FIFA president also defended the organization's ticket pricing strategy, noting that over six million tickets have been sold. He stated that the starting price of $60 is the lowest entry price for any American sports playoff phase and that revenue generated goes back into football development.

Looking ahead to the tournament, which kicks off at the historic Estadio Azteca, Infantino predicted a highly competitive event. He cited factors like altitude, climate, travel, and the expanded 48-team format as contributors to unpredictability, concluding with a call to 'let the celebration begin.'
caption:
FIFA President Gianni Infantino on visa issues: "We are not the kings of the world who can rule over governments and police forces."

FIFA President Gianni Infantino addressed concerns regarding visa issues and ticket prices on the eve of the World Cup. He urged stakeholders to 'chill and relax,' stating that FIFA, as a sports organization, cannot dictate immigration policies to host nations.

Infantino's comments came after a Somali referee was denied entry to the U.S. despite holding a valid visa. He acknowledged the unfortunate incident but stressed that FIFA is working behind the scenes to resolve such issues, emphasizing that immigration decisions ultimately rest with national authorities.

Despite controversies, Infantino expressed no regrets about the U.S. co-hosting the tournament, calling such issues 'normal for an event of this magnitude.' He highlighted Iran's participation as proof of FIFA's ability to navigate complex political landscapes and bring people together.

The FIFA president also defended the organization's ticket pricing strategy, noting that over six million tickets have been sold. He stated that the starting price of $60 is the lowest entry price for any American sports playoff phase and that revenue generated goes back into football development.

Looking ahead to the tournament, which kicks off at the historic Estadio Azteca, Infantino predicted a highly competitive event. He cited factors like altitude, climate, travel, and the expanded 48-team format as contributors to unpredictability, concluding with a call to 'let the celebration begin.'

Source: The Daily Star, Somoy News, Prothom Alo
